You're in — see you soon ♡

Who Is a Spoonie?

A spoonie is a person living with limited daily energy often due to chronic illness or nervous system disorders. People who use the term do so as a self-identifier and a shorthand for a shared experience. The word grew out of spoon theory, and a spoonie is someone who identifies as a member of the community.

  • Read the full explainer on Spoon Theory

Who Calls Themselves a Spoonie?

The most common spoonies are people living with chronic illness. That covers a long list of conditions, including fibromyalgia, ME/CFS, lupus, multiple sclerosis, EDS, POTS, long COVID, Lyme disease, autoimmune diseases, thyroid disease, diabetes, anemia, and many more.

Mental health conditions like depression, anxiety, ADHD, autism, and complex PTSD belong here too. Anything that puts a real limit on how much you can do in a day fits.

The term has also expanded beyond formal diagnoses. The chronic illness community has been generous about who gets to use the word, and the everyday reality is that lots of people live with chronic low energy from causes that aren’t strictly illness.

People who have ended up in the spoonie community include:

  • Caregivers, especially long-term ones
  • Cancer survivors
  • People recovering from major surgery or injury
  • New parents of medically complex babies
  • People in clinical burnout
  • People living with complex trauma
  • People navigating high-stress poverty

The common thread isn’t what’s wrong on paper. It’s that the person is operating with less daily energy than the world expects them to have, and they have to plan around it.

You Might Be a Spoonie If…

You don’t need a diagnosis to recognize yourself in this list. Most people in the community had a long stretch of suspecting they were a spoonie before any doctor confirmed it.

  • You’ve had to choose between a shower and making dinner
  • A single errand can wipe you out for the rest of the day
  • You plan your week around what you have to do, not what you want to do
  • “Resting” doesn’t restore you the way it seems to for healthy people
  • You’ve cancelled plans because your body said no, then felt guilty for the rest of the day
  • You know roughly how many good days you can count on in a month
  • Sleeping eight hours doesn’t mean you’ll wake up rested
  • You’ve said “I just need to lie down” more times than you can count

Why We Use This Word

Calling yourself a spoonie does three things at once.

First, it gives the experience legitimacy. There is a real difference between “I’m tired” and “I’m out of spoons,” and having a word that captures the difference matters. It signals that what you’re going through is not the normal tiredness everyone has.

Second, it’s shorthand. Instead of explaining a chronic condition, the spoon count system, your pacing strategy, and your boundary for the day, you can just say you’re low on spoons. A stranger may not get it. Another spoonie will get it immediately.

Third, it’s a way to find your people. Searching the word “spoonie” on Instagram, TikTok, Pinterest, or anywhere else turns up a community that doesn’t need the explanation. There’s a real relief in finding people who already know.

A Note on Community Etiquette

There is a quiet rule the community holds about who uses the term. Spoonie is meant for people whose daily energy is genuinely limited by illness, mental health, caregiving, recovery, or other persistent conditions. It is generally frowned upon for non-spoonies to use the framework to describe normal tiredness (“I’m heading to bed, I’m all out of spoons”), full schedules (“none of us have the spoons to start the project”), or frustration with someone’s behavior (“I don’t have the spoons for your attitude”).

This isn’t gatekeeping for the sake of it. It’s about protecting the meaning of a word that helps the people who actually need it be understood. If you’ve made it this far and you’re still not sure whether the label fits you, that’s normal. Most spoonies were not sure either.

Where to Go From Here

If you’re new to this community, or newly suspecting you might be part of it, the rest of the site is built for you. A few starting places:

  • Easy 2 and 3-ingredient recipes for low-spoon days
  • Lifehacks for when standing isn’t on the menu
  • Honest product recommendations from a working spoonie household
  • Subscribe to the weekly roundup

And if you got here without reading the metaphor itself, start there:

  • What Is Spoon Theory?

Photo by Ahmet Sali on Unsplash

Hi, I'm Twila.

I created No Spoons Wasted as a resource for fellow spoonies navigating life with low energy. The blog provides recipes, strategies, and products for the days when you just can't.
